text: The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection in a case of diverticulitis with a colocutaneous fistula, using the adherence of the phlegmon as a self-retaining retractor. The dissection proceeds underneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery, with careful identification of the ureter located laterally. The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, considering the preservation of the left colic artery due to the patient's smoking history, and continues the medial dissection.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection above the phlegmon, aiming to improve mobility by positioning the sigmoid colon on a swivel. The dissection progresses towards the medial plane previously dissected, moving down the left side towards the adherence of the phlegmon. Firefly imaging is used to identify the ureter, aiding in precise dissection.

The surgeon approaches the abscess cavity head-on in the midline, attempting to bluntly separate the surrounding tissue. Using left-handed dissection with a suction device, the surgeon clears blood and smoke while acting as a retractor and blunt dissector. The abscess cavity is entered, revealing the IR drain.

The surgeon works to separate the abscess cavity from the anterior abdominal wall, revealing an epithelialized mucosal surface indicative of a giant diverticulum. The pouch of mucosa is visible below the drain, which is being removed to improve access. The posterior wall of the diverticulum is exposed, and the surgeon attempts to dissect through it to achieve blunt separation.

The surgeon attempts to dissect beneath the adherence of the phlegmon but finds it challenging. They return to the lateral side to clean up and access the previously dissected medial plane. A sponge is visible in the surgical field. The surgeon aims to mobilize the sigmoid colon by swinging it to the patient's right, hoping to gain more mobility and safely dissect in known planes without inadvertently entering the bladder.

The surgeon encounters oozing while dissecting, carefully cleaning the area. The dissection progresses as the surgeon inadvertently enters the colon, revealing the correct plane above it. The surgeon continues to dissect, pulling down peritoneum and preperitoneal fat, exposing the iliac and ureter. Using a ligature, the surgeon frees the lateral attachments, performing a lateral mesorectal excision despite the messy conditions.

The surgeon has freed the entire specimen and lifted it out of the pelvis, completing a full splenic flexure mobilization. The lateral approach reveals the base of the pancreas, and the colon is lifted off of it. The surgeon assesses the reach and clears the margins proximally and distally, entering the lesser sac.

The surgeon uses ICG Firefly imaging to assess perfusion before transecting the proximal margin of the colon. The surgical field is prepared for clearing the distal margin, with visible tissue manipulation and instrument use.

The surgeon transects the distal margin slightly lower than the cleared mesentery using a vessel sealer. A hand-sewn single-layer anastomosis is performed, with two 3-0 silk sutures placed for stability: one on the far left side and one in the posterior midline. The surgeon sutures around the far corner towards the left side, completing the posterior row.

The surgeon begins the second suture in the posterior midline, overlapping the first stitch, and sews towards the right side in the posterior row. Blood from the phlegmon smears the camera, but the surgeon continues suturing despite the dirty lens. The suturing progresses to the anterior, where a canal suture is placed across the top to invert the lip. The surgeon completes the suturing and ties the two sutures together, assessing the anastomosis as intact before performing a leak test.

The surgeon passes a 19mm rigid proctor stroke through the anastomosis, confirming its wide patency. Methylene blue saline solution is used to insufflate the bladder, ensuring no bladder fistula or injury occurred during dissection, with no extravasation observed. A drain is left in place, and the Foley catheter is retained for 48 hours due to bladder adherence.
